%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Learn the Heaviside Cover-Up Method — a fast, elegant shortcut for finding partial fraction coefficients without solving simultaneous equations.

When decomposing a rational function \(F(x) = P(x)/Q(x)\) into partial fractions, the brute-force approach requires solving a system of equations. For large denominators, this becomes tedious. The **Heaviside Cover-Up Method** eliminates that work for cases where \(Q(x)\) has **distinct** (non-repeated) factors.

The core idea: to find the coefficient \(k_i\) for a factor \((x - a_i)\), simply **cover up** that factor in the denominator and substitute \(x = a_i\) into the remaining expression. After this section, you will be able to find partial fraction coefficients quickly and confidently — for both real and complex factors.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The Heaviside Cover-Up Method in three steps: write the partial fraction form, cover one denominator factor, substitute its root into the remaining expression to read off the coefficient directly.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777216907937-1248.png)

## Core Idea

Suppose you have a proper rational function with **distinct** denominator factors:

$$
F(x) = \frac{2x^2 + 9x - 11}{(x+1)(x-2)(x+3)} = \frac{k_1}{x+1} + \frac{k_2}{x-2} + \frac{k_3}{x+3}
$$

To isolate \(k_1\), multiply both sides by \((x+1)\) and set \(x = -1\). The right side collapses to \(k_1\) because every other term has \((x+1)\) in its denominator, which vanishes. The left side becomes:

$$
k_1 = \left.\frac{2x^2 + 9x - 11}{(x-2)(x+3)}\right|_{x=-1} = \frac{2(1) + 9(-1) - 11}{(-3)(2)} = \frac{-18}{-6} = 3
$$

**The cover-up shortcut:** instead of multiplying out, just mentally cover \((x+1)\) in the denominator of \(F(x)\) with your finger, then substitute \(x = -1\) into what remains. Repeat for each factor.

### COMMON EXAM TRAP

Always substitute the **root of the factor you covered** — for factor \((x + a)\), substitute \(x = -a\), not \(x = +a\). Mixing up the sign is the single most frequent error on exams.

### KEY INSIGHT

This method works for **complex** factors too. Factor \(x^2 + 4x + 13\) into \((x+2-j3)(x+2+j3)\) and apply the same cover-up procedure at \(x = -2+j3\) and \(x = -2-j3\).

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Cover one denominator factor, substitute its root — the coefficient appears directly with no equation system needed.
- The substitution value is always the root of the covered factor: for \((x+a)\), use \(x = -a\).
- The method extends to complex factors by factoring quadratics and applying the same cover-up steps.

*In the next section we will handle **repeated factors** in \(Q(x)\), where the cover-up method must be adapted and differentiation enters the picture.*
